questions.question: Which of the following correctly ranks the intermolecular forces present in liquid water from strongest to weakest?

choices.text: London dispersion > hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole
choices.explanation: In water, hydrogen bonding is the dominant intermolecular force; London dispersion forces are weakest.

choices.text: Hydrogen bonding > dipole-dipole > London dispersion
choices.explanation: Water molecules form strong hydrogen bonds (O–H···O); weaker dipole-dipole interactions and weakest London dispersion forces also exist, but H-bonding dominates.

choices.text: Dipole-dipole > hydrogen bonding > London dispersion
choices.explanation: Hydrogen bonds are a special, stronger subset of dipole interactions; they are stronger than ordinary dipole-dipole forces.

choices.text: London dispersion > dipole-dipole > hydrogen bonding
choices.explanation: This ranking is completely reversed; hydrogen bonding is the strongest type among these three for water molecules.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the boiling point of $\text{HF}$ is higher than that of $\text{HCl}$, even though $\text{HCl}$ has a higher molar mass?

choices.text: $\text{HF}$ forms hydrogen bonds between molecules; $\text{HCl}$ cannot form hydrogen bonds because chlorine is not electronegative enough
choices.explanation: Fluorine's high electronegativity and small size make H–F···F hydrogen bonds strong enough to significantly elevate the boiling point despite the lower molar mass of HF.

choices.text: $\text{HF}$ is more polar than $\text{HCl}$, so it has stronger London dispersion forces
choices.explanation: Greater polarity increases dipole-dipole forces, not London dispersion forces; the key difference is hydrogen bonding in HF, not London dispersion.

choices.text: $\text{HF}$ has a larger dipole moment, which creates stronger ionic bonds between molecules
choices.explanation: Intermolecular forces in HF are not ionic; they are hydrogen bonds and dipole-dipole interactions.

choices.text: $\text{HCl}$ has stronger London dispersion forces because of its larger electron cloud, which should lower its boiling point
choices.explanation: Larger electron clouds do increase London dispersion forces, which would tend to raise the boiling point of HCl, not lower it; however, HF's hydrogen bonding still wins out overall.

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following best explains the trend in boiling points for the straight-chain alkane series?

choices.text: As molar mass increases, hydrogen bonding becomes stronger, raising the boiling point
choices.explanation: Alkanes are nonpolar and cannot form hydrogen bonds; the trend is due to increasing London dispersion forces.

choices.text: As molar mass increases, the London dispersion forces become stronger due to larger, more polarizable electron clouds
choices.explanation: Larger molecules have more electrons and greater surface area, allowing stronger temporary dipole fluctuations and therefore stronger London dispersion forces, increasing boiling points.

choices.text: As molar mass increases, dipole-dipole forces increase because alkane molecules become more polar
choices.explanation: Alkanes remain nonpolar regardless of chain length; they cannot have dipole-dipole forces.

choices.text: Boiling point increases because longer chains have stronger covalent bonds that require more energy to break
choices.explanation: Boiling involves overcoming intermolecular forces, not covalent bonds; covalent bonds are not broken when a liquid boils.

questions.question: Iodine ($\text{I}_2$) is a nonpolar molecule yet is much more soluble in nonpolar $\text{CCl}_4$ than in polar water. Which of the following best explains this observation?

choices.text: Iodine cannot form hydrogen bonds with water, and water molecules prefer to maintain their hydrogen bond network rather than solvate a nonpolar solute
choices.explanation: Water molecules form an extensive hydrogen bond network; incorporating nonpolar I₂ would disrupt this network with no compensating H-bond formation, making dissolution energetically unfavorable.

choices.text: Iodine is too large to fit in the spaces between water molecules
choices.explanation: While iodine is large, size alone does not account for the solubility difference; the type of intermolecular interactions is the primary factor.

choices.text: Iodine reacts chemically with $\text{CCl}_4$ to form a new compound but not with water
choices.explanation: Iodine dissolves physically in CCl₄; the process is solvation by London dispersion forces, not a chemical reaction.

choices.text: The density of iodine is greater than that of water, causing it to sink rather than dissolve
choices.explanation: Density determines sinking/floating but is unrelated to solubility; a dense solid can dissolve in a less dense solvent.

title.text: Vapor Pressure vs. Mole Fraction (Component A)

questions.question: The graph shows the vapor pressure of a two-component solution compared to an ideal solution (Raoult's law). Which of the following best interprets the positive deviation from Raoult's law shown in the graph?

choices.text: The intermolecular forces between molecules of A and B are stronger than those between like molecules, reducing vapor pressure
choices.explanation: Stronger A–B interactions than A–A or B–B would produce a negative deviation (lower vapor pressure than ideal), not a positive deviation.

choices.text: The intermolecular forces between molecules of A and B are weaker than those between like molecules, increasing vapor pressure above the ideal prediction
choices.explanation: When A–B interactions are weaker than A–A and B–B, molecules escape the solution more easily, producing higher vapor pressure than Raoult's law predicts (positive deviation).

choices.text: The solution has a higher boiling point than expected because molecules are harder to vaporize
choices.explanation: Higher vapor pressure corresponds to lower, not higher, boiling points; positive deviation means molecules escape more readily.

choices.text: The positive deviation indicates the solution is an ideal mixture following Raoult's law exactly
choices.explanation: An ideal solution would follow Raoult's law exactly (dashed line); the positive deviation shows the observed vapor pressure is above the ideal prediction.

questions.question: A student dissolves 5.85 g of $\text{NaCl}$ (molar mass = 58.5 g/mol) in enough water to make 500. mL of solution. What is the molarity of the $\text{NaCl}$ solution?

choices.text: 0.100 mol/L
choices.explanation: 0.100 mol/L would result from dividing 0.1 mol by 1.0 L; with 500. mL = 0.500 L, the molarity is 0.200 mol/L.

choices.text: 0.200 mol/L
choices.explanation: Moles of NaCl = 5.85 g ÷ 58.5 g/mol = 0.100 mol. Molarity = 0.100 mol ÷ 0.500 L = 0.200 mol/L.

choices.text: 0.500 mol/L
choices.explanation: 0.500 mol/L would require 0.250 mol in 500. mL; the sample contains only 0.100 mol.
This would require only 0.005 mol; the sample contains 0.100 mol, giving 0.200 mol/L.

questions.question: Which of the following pairs of liquids would be expected to be miscible based on the principle 'like dissolves like'?

choices.text: Water and hexane
choices.explanation: Water is polar and hexane is nonpolar; their intermolecular forces are incompatible and they will not mix (immiscible).

choices.text: Ethanol and water
choices.explanation: Ethanol has an –OH group capable of hydrogen bonding with water; their similar polarity and H-bonding capability make them miscible in all proportions.

choices.text: Oil and water
choices.explanation: Oil is nonpolar; water is polar. They are immiscible due to incompatible intermolecular forces.

choices.text: Benzene and water
choices.explanation: Benzene is nonpolar and aromatic; water is polar. They are immiscible.

stimulus.content: The Beer-Lambert Law states that absorbance ($A$) is proportional to the molar absorptivity ($\varepsilon$), path length ($l$), and concentration ($c$): $A = \varepsilon l c$.

questions.question: A student measures the absorbance of three solutions of the same dye in a 1.0 cm cuvette: 0.10 mol/L gives A = 0.23; 0.20 mol/L gives A = 0.46; 0.30 mol/L gives A = 0.69. Which of the following conclusions is best supported by this data?

choices.text: The data support the Beer-Lambert Law because absorbance doubles when concentration doubles
choices.explanation: The data show a linear relationship: A is directly proportional to concentration (0.23 per 0.10 mol/L). This is precisely what Beer-Lambert Law predicts.

choices.text: The data do not follow Beer-Lambert Law because absorbance does not change by a fixed amount per unit concentration
choices.explanation: The absorbance increases by exactly 0.23 for each 0.10 mol/L increase, showing a consistent linear relationship consistent with Beer-Lambert Law.

choices.text: The molar absorptivity cannot be calculated from this data because path length is not given
choices.explanation: Path length is given as 1.0 cm; using $\varepsilon = A/(lc)$, the molar absorptivity can be calculated directly.

choices.text: The data suggest that higher concentrations absorb more light due to increased molecular weight of the solution
choices.explanation: Beer-Lambert Law relates absorbance to concentration, not molecular weight; the increase is due to more absorbing molecules in the light path, not molecular weight change.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the kinetic molecular theory postulate that explains why gases have much lower densities than liquids?

choices.text: Gas molecules move faster than liquid molecules, reducing collision frequency
choices.explanation: Speed alone does not explain density; it is the large average distance between gas molecules that accounts for lower density.

choices.text: Gas molecules are much farther apart than liquid molecules, so gas occupies a much larger volume per unit mass
choices.explanation: KMT postulates that the volume of gas molecules is negligible compared to the container volume; molecules are far apart, giving gases very low densities.

choices.text: Gas molecules are smaller than liquid molecules and therefore take up less space
choices.explanation: The same molecule (e.g., H₂O) can be liquid or gas; the molecules themselves do not change size between phases.

choices.text: Intermolecular forces are stronger in gases, pulling molecules together and reducing density
choices.explanation: Intermolecular forces are negligible between gas molecules; the weak or absent intermolecular forces allow gas molecules to spread apart.

questions.question: A sample of ideal gas in a sealed rigid container is heated from 300 K to 600 K. Which of the following correctly predicts the change in pressure?

choices.text: Pressure doubles because pressure is directly proportional to absolute temperature at constant volume
choices.explanation: Gay-Lussac's Law (from the ideal gas law at constant n and V): $P \propto T$. Doubling T from 300 K to 600 K doubles P.

choices.text: Pressure doubles because volume decreases when temperature increases
choices.explanation: The container is rigid (constant volume); volume does not change. The pressure change is due to Gay-Lussac's Law, not Boyle's Law.

choices.text: Pressure remains constant because the number of molecules is constant
choices.explanation: Pressure at constant volume does change with temperature; more kinetic energy means more forceful and frequent wall collisions, increasing pressure.

choices.text: Pressure increases by a factor of 4 because kinetic energy is proportional to $T^2$
choices.explanation: Average kinetic energy is proportional to T (absolute temperature), not T²; pressure at constant volume is directly proportional to T, so doubling T doubles P.

title.text: Maxwell-Boltzmann: Fraction of Molecules vs. Speed (m/s)

questions.question: The graph shows Maxwell-Boltzmann speed distributions for $\text{N}_2$ and $\text{Xe}$ at the same temperature. Which of the following best explains why the $\text{N}_2$ curve peaks at a higher speed than the $\text{Xe}$ curve?

choices.text: $\text{N}_2$ molecules are smaller and can fit through spaces more easily
choices.explanation: Molecular size is not the relevant factor; it is the mass of the molecules that determines average speed at a given temperature.

choices.text: At the same temperature, all gas molecules have the same average kinetic energy; lighter $\text{N}_2$ molecules must travel faster to have the same kinetic energy as heavier $\text{Xe}$ molecules
choices.explanation: Since $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ and average KE is equal at the same temperature, lighter molecules have higher average speeds: $v \propto 1/\sqrt{m}$.

choices.text: $\text{N}_2$ is diatomic and has more internal energy from rotation, giving it higher translational speeds
choices.explanation: Rotational energy is separate from translational kinetic energy; the Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution of translational speeds is determined by mass, not molecular complexity.

choices.text: $\text{Xe}$ is a noble gas with stronger London dispersion forces, slowing it down
choices.explanation: In the gas phase, intermolecular forces are negligible and do not significantly affect the Maxwell-Boltzmann speed distribution.

questions.question: Which of the following gases would be expected to deviate most from ideal gas behavior at high pressure and low temperature?

choices.text: $\text{H}_2$
choices.explanation: H₂ has very weak London dispersion forces (smallest molecule), making it behave most ideally among the choices.

choices.text: $\text{He}$
choices.explanation: Helium is a noble gas with negligible intermolecular forces, behaving very close to an ideal gas.

choices.text: $\text{NH}_3$
choices.explanation: NH₃ has strong hydrogen bonding (significant intermolecular forces) and a larger molecular volume; at high pressure and low temperature, these factors cause large deviations from ideal gas behavior.

choices.text: $\text{N}_2$
choices.explanation: N₂ has relatively weak London dispersion forces and behaves more ideally than NH₃ under most conditions.

stimulus.content: A chemist uses paper chromatography to separate a mixture of food dyes. After developing the chromatogram with a water/ethanol solvent, four colored spots appear at different positions. The solvent front travels 8.0 cm from the baseline, and the centers of the spots are at 6.4 cm, 4.8 cm, 3.2 cm, and 1.6 cm from the baseline.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies which dye has the greatest affinity for the mobile phase (solvent) relative to the stationary phase (paper)?

choices.text: The dye at 1.6 cm, because it traveled the shortest distance
choices.explanation: A spot with a small $R_f$ value moved the least, indicating stronger affinity for the stationary phase (paper), not the mobile phase (solvent).

choices.text: The dye at 6.4 cm, because it has the highest $R_f$ value and moved farthest with the solvent
choices.explanation: $R_f = d_{spot}/d_{solvent} = 6.4/8.0 = 0.80$; the highest $R_f$ indicates the greatest affinity for the mobile phase relative to the stationary phase.

choices.text: All dyes have the same affinity for the mobile phase because they were all carried by the same solvent
choices.explanation: Different affinities for stationary vs. mobile phase are precisely what separate the dyes; if affinities were equal, all spots would be at the same position.

choices.text: The dye at 4.8 cm, because it is in the middle of the chromatogram, representing the average affinity
choices.explanation: Chromatographic separation is not based on averages; the spot that traveled farthest has the greatest affinity for the mobile phase.

questions.question: A student uses a spectrophotometer to determine the concentration of a colored solution. The student prepares standards and plots absorbance vs. concentration, obtaining a straight line. Which of the following best describes the purpose of the calibration curve?

choices.text: To verify that the spectrophotometer is producing light of the correct wavelength
choices.explanation: A calibration curve relates absorbance to concentration for a specific solute; it does not verify the wavelength of light produced by the instrument.

choices.text: To establish a linear relationship between absorbance and concentration so that an unknown concentration can be determined from its measured absorbance
choices.explanation: The calibration curve (based on Beer-Lambert Law) allows interpolation: measuring absorbance of an unknown and reading off its concentration from the linear relationship.

choices.text: To determine the molecular weight of the solute by comparing absorbance values
choices.explanation: Spectrophotometry measures concentration, not molecular weight; molecular weight must be known to calculate molar absorptivity.

choices.text: To ensure the solution is homogeneous before measurement
choices.explanation: Homogeneity is ensured by proper mixing before measurement; the calibration curve is used for quantitative analysis.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why surface tension of water is higher than that of most organic liquids?

choices.text: Water molecules are smaller and pack more tightly, increasing surface tension
choices.explanation: While small size contributes, the primary explanation is the strong hydrogen bonding network that creates high cohesive forces at the surface.

choices.text: Water has stronger hydrogen bonding that creates high cohesive forces, making the surface resistant to deformation
choices.explanation: Surface tension arises from the net inward attraction on surface molecules; water's extensive hydrogen bonding creates strong cohesion, resulting in high surface tension.

choices.text: Water is denser than most organic liquids, requiring more force to stretch the surface
choices.explanation: Density and surface tension are not directly correlated; surface tension depends on intermolecular forces, not bulk density.

choices.text: Organic liquids have weaker van der Waals forces, which create stronger surface tension
choices.explanation: Weaker forces would create lower, not higher, surface tension; water's stronger H-bonding gives it higher surface tension than organic liquids with weaker forces.

questions.question: An unknown substance is a hard, brittle solid that melts at 1400°C and does not conduct electricity in either solid or liquid form. Based on the table, which type of solid does this substance most likely represent?

choices.text: Ionic solid
choices.explanation: Ionic solids conduct electricity when melted; the unknown substance does not conduct in liquid form, ruling out ionic.

choices.text: Molecular solid
choices.explanation: Molecular solids have low melting points; a melting point of 1400°C is inconsistent with a molecular solid held by weak intermolecular forces.

choices.text: Covalent network solid
choices.explanation: Covalent network solids have very high melting points, do not conduct electricity in any state, and are very hard (though can be brittle); all characteristics match the unknown substance.

choices.text: Metallic solid
choices.explanation: Metallic solids conduct electricity in both solid and liquid form; the unknown does not conduct, eliminating metallic solid.

questions.question: A 1.0 mol/L aqueous glucose solution and a 1.0 mol/L aqueous $\text{NaCl}$ solution are prepared. Which of the following best explains why the NaCl solution has a lower freezing point than the glucose solution?

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$ has a higher molar mass than glucose, so it is more effective at lowering the freezing point
choices.explanation: Freezing point depression depends on the number of dissolved particles (molality), not molar mass; NaCl produces more particles per formula unit.

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$ dissociates into two ions ($\text{Na}^+$ and $\text{Cl}^-$) per formula unit, producing twice as many dissolved particles as glucose, which does not dissociate
choices.explanation: Freezing point depression is a colligative property proportional to particle concentration. 1 mol NaCl → 2 mol particles; 1 mol glucose → 1 mol particles. More particles = greater depression.

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$ is ionic and therefore has stronger interactions with water, pulling water molecules into a more ordered structure
choices.explanation: Stronger solute-water interactions describe solvation, not the mechanism of freezing point depression; the key factor is particle count.

choices.text: Glucose is larger than NaCl, and larger molecules are less effective at disrupting the ice crystal lattice
choices.explanation: Freezing point depression is determined by the number of solute particles, not their size; the mechanism involves disruption of solvent-solvent interactions.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between temperature and the rate of effusion of a gas?

choices.text: Rate of effusion is inversely proportional to temperature because higher temperatures increase collisions with the container walls
choices.explanation: Higher temperatures increase molecular speed, which increases effusion rate; the relationship is a direct (not inverse) one.

choices.text: Rate of effusion is proportional to $\sqrt{T}$ because average molecular speed is proportional to $\sqrt{T}$
choices.explanation: From kinetic molecular theory, $v_{rms} = \sqrt{3RT/M}$, so $v \propto \sqrt{T}$. Since effusion rate depends on how fast molecules reach the opening, rate $\propto \sqrt{T}$.

choices.text: Rate of effusion is proportional to $T^2$ because kinetic energy increases with the square of temperature
choices.explanation: Average kinetic energy is proportional to T (not T²), and effusion rate is proportional to speed (which goes as $\sqrt{T}$), not kinetic energy directly.

choices.text: Rate of effusion is independent of temperature; only molar mass affects effusion
choices.explanation: Graham's Law compares effusion rates of two gases at the same temperature; temperature absolutely affects effusion rate as described by KMT.

stimulus.content: A chemist dissolves $\text{CaCl}_2$ in water. The van 't Hoff factor ($i$) for $\text{CaCl}_2$ is experimentally found to be 2.7 rather than the theoretical value of 3.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why the experimental van 't Hoff factor is less than 3?

choices.text: $\text{CaCl}_2$ does not fully dissolve in water
choices.explanation: $\text{CaCl}_2$ is highly soluble; incomplete dissolution is not the explanation for i < 3 in dilute solutions.

choices.text: Ion pairing — electrostatic attractions between oppositely charged ions cause some ions to associate in solution, reducing the effective particle count
choices.explanation: At finite concentrations, $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ and $\text{Cl}^-$ ions attract each other, forming temporary ion pairs that behave as single particles, reducing the effective particle count below the theoretical 3.

choices.text: $\text{CaCl}_2$ reacts with water to form a new compound, reducing the number of ions
choices.explanation: $\text{CaCl}_2$ dissolves to give ions without reacting chemically with water (it does not hydrolyze significantly); the effect is ion pairing, not chemical reaction.

choices.text: The calcium ion forms a complex with chloride ions, producing fewer particles than expected
choices.explanation: While this describes a similar mechanism to ion pairing, true complex formation would produce a stoichiometrically defined complex; the actual effect in dilute solutions is transient ion pairing.

questions.question: A chemist calculates the number of moles of $\text{CO}_2$ at STP. Which of the following correctly uses the ideal gas law to find the volume occupied by 2.0 mol of $\text{CO}_2$ at STP (T = 273 K, P = 1.00 atm, R = 0.0821 L·atm/mol·K)?

choices.explanation: 11.2 L is the volume for 0.5 mol, not 2.0 mol; using V = nRT/P = (0.5)(0.0821)(273)/1.00 = 11.2 L.
22.4 L is the molar volume for 1.0 mol at STP; for 2.0 mol the volume doubles to 44.8 L.
V = nRT/P = (2.0 mol)(0.0821 L·atm/mol·K)(273 K)/(1.00 atm) = 44.8 L, which is twice the molar volume at STP.
89.6 L would be the volume for 4.0 mol at STP; dividing 89.6 L by 4.0 mol gives 22.4 L/mol, confirming 2.0 mol = 44.8 L.

questions.question: Based on the data in the table, which of the following best explains why ethanol (78°C) has a higher boiling point than dimethyl ether (−24°C) despite having the same molecular formula ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$)?

choices.text: Ethanol has a higher molar mass than dimethyl ether
choices.explanation: They are isomers with the same molecular formula ($\text{C}_2\text{H}_6\text{O}$) and therefore identical molar mass.

choices.text: Ethanol has an O–H group that can form hydrogen bonds between molecules; dimethyl ether cannot donate hydrogen bonds
choices.explanation: Ethanol's –OH group acts as both hydrogen bond donor and acceptor; dimethyl ether can only accept H-bonds (via oxygen lone pairs) but cannot donate. The additional H-bonding in ethanol dramatically raises its boiling point.

choices.text: Ethanol is a liquid while dimethyl ether is a gas at room temperature, so they cannot be directly compared
choices.explanation: Boiling point is a fixed physical property that can be directly compared between substances regardless of their room-temperature state.

choices.text: Dimethyl ether is symmetric and its dipoles cancel, making it nonpolar
choices.explanation: Dimethyl ether is polar (bent geometry around oxygen); its lower boiling point results from inability to donate H-bonds, not nonpolarity.

questions.question: Graham's Law of Effusion states that the rate of effusion is inversely proportional to the square root of molar mass. If $\text{H}_2$ effuses at a rate of 4.0 mol/min, at what rate will $\text{O}_2$ effuse under the same conditions?

choices.text: 0.25 mol/min
choices.explanation: 0.25 is 4.0/16, not the correct ratio; the ratio of rates is $\sqrt{M_{O_2}/M_{H_2}} = \sqrt{32/2} = \sqrt{16} = 4$.

choices.text: 0.50 mol/min
choices.explanation: 0.50 = 4.0/8; this would correspond to a mass ratio of 64, not 16; $\sqrt{M_{O_2}/M_{H_2}} = \sqrt{32/2} = 4$, giving $r_{O_2} = 4.0/4 = 1.0$ mol/min.

choices.text: 1.0 mol/min
2.0 mol/min
choices.explanation: 2.0 would correspond to a rate ratio of 2, implying $\sqrt{M_{O_2}/M_{H_2}} = 2$, i.e., $M_{O_2}/M_{H_2} = 4$; the actual ratio is 16.

stimulus.content: A student compares the conductivity of three aqueous solutions: 0.1 mol/L $\text{NaCl}$, 0.1 mol/L glucose, and 0.1 mol/L acetic acid ($\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$). The conductivity meter reads: NaCl = 1.26 mS/cm, acetic acid = 0.052 mS/cm, glucose = 0.0005 mS/cm.

questions.question: Based on the conductivity data, which of the following best classifies the three solutes?

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$: strong electrolyte; $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: weak electrolyte; glucose: nonelectrolyte
choices.explanation: NaCl dissociates completely (high conductivity), acetic acid dissociates partially (low but measurable conductivity), and glucose does not produce ions (negligible conductivity).

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$: strong electrolyte; $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: nonelectrolyte; glucose: weak electrolyte
choices.explanation: Acetic acid has measurable conductivity, indicating it produces some ions (weak electrolyte); glucose's conductivity is negligible, making it the nonelectrolyte.

choices.text: All three are electrolytes because they all show some conductivity
choices.explanation: Glucose conductivity (0.0005 mS/cm) is essentially zero and attributable to water's autoionization; glucose is classified as a nonelectrolyte.

choices.text: $\text{NaCl}$: weak electrolyte; $\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$: strong electrolyte; glucose: nonelectrolyte
choices.explanation: NaCl completely dissociates (strong electrolyte, highest conductivity); acetic acid only partially dissociates (weak electrolyte, much lower conductivity).

questions.question: Which of the following correctly explains why gases at high pressure deviate from ideal gas behavior?

choices.text: At high pressure, gas molecules move faster, increasing the frequency of collisions with the walls
choices.explanation: Increased collision frequency at high pressure does approximate one aspect of gas behavior, but deviation from ideal gas law is due to finite molecular volume becoming significant, not speed.

choices.text: At high pressure, the finite volume of gas molecules becomes a significant fraction of the container volume, causing the actual volume to be greater than predicted by ideal gas law
choices.explanation: Ideal gas law assumes molecules have zero volume. At high pressure, molecules are packed closely and their actual volume is not negligible; the measured volume exceeds the ideal prediction.

choices.text: At high pressure, intermolecular forces become negligible, causing molecules to behave more randomly
choices.explanation: This is reversed; at high pressure, molecules are closer together and intermolecular forces are more significant, increasing deviation from ideal behavior.

choices.text: At high pressure, the mass of gas molecules increases due to compression
choices.explanation: Compression does not change the mass of molecules; the deviation from ideal behavior is due to molecular volume and intermolecular forces, not mass changes.

questions.question: A solution of sucrose is separated from pure water by a semipermeable membrane. In which direction will solvent flow, and what is this process called?

choices.text: Water flows from the sucrose solution into the pure water; this is osmosis
choices.explanation: Water flows from lower solute concentration (pure water) to higher solute concentration (sucrose solution); flow toward pure water would be reverse osmosis.

choices.text: Water flows from pure water into the sucrose solution; this is osmosis
choices.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of solvent through a semipermeable membrane from lower solute concentration to higher solute concentration, diluting the more concentrated solution.

choices.text: Sucrose flows from the solution into the pure water; this is diffusion
choices.explanation: The membrane is semipermeable; large sucrose molecules cannot pass through it. Only solvent (water) passes through in osmosis.

choices.text: No net flow occurs because concentration equilibrium is already established at the membrane
choices.explanation: There is a concentration gradient across the membrane; net water flow will continue until the osmotic pressure is balanced by the hydrostatic pressure difference.
